subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: Congruence is the whole subject of the chapter: it defines congruent figures as exact copies that superimpose, and section 9.2 builds up the triangle-congruence conditions (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, RHS) and the reasons SSA and AAA fail, so students learn to identify congruent triangles and express the congruence correctly — precisely the understanding of congruence as it applies to geometric shapes.

Section 9.3 derives and uses the angle properties of triangles — angles opposite equal sides are equal, and every angle of an equilateral triangle is 60° — and the Figure it Out sets apply triangle and angle properties to find missing angles, show angle bisection, and establish parallelism, which is the outlining and applying of the properties of triangles and angles to solve related problems.

adjunct_competencies.justification: The chapter is built on deductive argument: it justifies each congruence condition, proves the isosceles-angle result using the RHS condition and corresponding parts, and repeatedly asks students to 'show that', 'explain why', and 'give reasons' (e.g. proving AB ∥ CD from congruent triangles), producing convincing geometric arguments.

Several tasks call for creative discovery of one's own solutions — finding the different ways a square's diagonal makes congruent triangles, and giving an example of two triangles congruent to each other in six different ways — inviting students to search for and appreciate multiple valid correspondences.

The triangle constructions are presented as ordered, repeatable procedures (draw the base, draw the angle line, cut an arc of given radius, join the point), and the case-by-case testing of SSS/SAS/SSA/ASA/RHS follows a systematic step-wise method, engaging algorithmic, ordered-step thinking.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to determine whether two triangles are congruent by applying the SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, and RHS conditions and reasoning from corresponding parts, and to deduce the angle properties of isosceles and equilateral triangles.
